mnemonics.title: TYPES OF ACCESS METHODS
mnemonics.content: S DIR: SEQUENTIAL, DIRECT/INDEXED, RELATIVE - Helps remember the three main categories in order of complexity.

mnemonics.title: FILE ALLOCATION PROBLEMS
mnemonics.content: E I E I O - External, Internal fragmentation, External, Index overhead, Oh (none perfect!) - Remembering issues with different allocation methods.

mnemonics.title: DISK ACCESS TIME COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: SARA: Seek time, Access latency (rotational), Transfer time, All combined - Four main components of disk access time.

mnemonics.title: CONTIGUOUS VS LINKED ALLOCATION
mnemonics.content: CONTIGUOUS: CONsecutive blocks - FAST for both Sequential and Direct but FRAGMENTED. LINKED: LInked blocks - No Fragmentation but SLOW Direct access.

acronyms.usage: A data structure used in linked allocation that stores pointers to the next block of each file.
A data structure that stores all information about a file including its location, size, and access permissions.
A disk scheduling algorithm that processes requests in the order they arrive, related to access optimization.
A storage technology that combines multiple disk drives for reliability and performance.
A disk scheduling algorithm that minimizes head movement by servicing the nearest request first.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a movie film strip where you must watch frames in order from start to finish - you cannot jump directly to frame 50 without through frames 1 passing-49.
Think of a book with page numbers - you can open directly to any page without reading previous pages, just like a disk's read/write head jumps to any track.
Picture a library's card catalog or book's index - you look up a topic in the index (index block) which tells you exactly which page (data block) contains the information.
Think of a treasure hunt where each clue (disk block) points to the location of the next clue - you must follow the chain sequentially to find the treasure.
Imagine a parking lot with continuous spaces - a long vehicle (file) needs consecutive spaces; if scattered, even with enough total spaces, it cannot park.
Think of a table of contents in a textbook - it lists chapter starting pages, allowing you to jump directly to any chapter without reading previous ones.

analogies.analogy: Like listening to a cassette tape - to hear song 5, you must fast-forward through songs 1-4. No skipping allowed!
Like a DVD player - you can press a button to jump directly to any chapter or scene without watching previous ones.
Like a scavenger hunt where each location has a clue pointing to the next location - you must follow the chain in order.
Like a dictionary with alphabetical tabs - the tabs (index) help you jump directly to any letter section without scanning the entire book.
Like booking adjacent seats in a theater - efficient for group access but becomes impossible as seats get scattered.
Like having small coins scattered in different pockets - you have enough total money but cannot make a large purchase because no single location has enough.
